text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY / MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD CONSULTATION NOTE

ATTENDING: Head and Neck Medical Oncology, Radiation Oncology (co-attending)
CONSULTATION TYPE: New patient, multidisciplinary tumor board evaluation
Referring provider: Otolaryngology / Head and Neck Surgery

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old female, never-smoker, who presents for multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment planning of a newly diagnosed p16-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue with right ipsilateral neck adenopathy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old West African-descent woman who works as a high school music teacher. She presented to her primary care physician approximately 3 months prior to this evaluation with a complaint of progressive right-sided throat pain with swallowing and a palpable right-sided neck lump. She had attributed the discomfort initially to a sore throat but noted no improvement over several weeks. She denied any fevers, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss at the time of initial presentation.

She was referred to otolaryngology, where oropharyngoscopy revealed an exophytic, friable, vascular mass arising from the right base of tongue. She underwent direct laryngoscopy with biopsy under general anesthesia. Pathology returned as invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, with p16 strongly and diffusely positive immunostaining (>75% of cells, block-like nuclear and cytoplasmic pattern), consistent with transcriptionally active HPV infection. Concurrent HPV RNA in situ hybridization confirmed high-risk HPV type 16 expression in tumor cells. Fine needle aspiration of the palpable right level II neck mass was also positive for metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, p16 positive.

Staging workup was completed as follows:

- MRI neck with and without contrast: 3.2 x 2.8 cm enhancing mass at the right base of tongue without extension to the vallecula or pre-epiglottic space; single right level IIA lymph node 2.1 x 1.8 cm with central necrosis and peripheral enhancement. No contralateral adenopathy, no retropharyngeal nodes, no bony involvement, no perineural invasion on imaging.

- PET-CT (18F-FDG): FDG-avid right base of tongue primary (SUVmax 14.2) with single FDG-avid right level IIA node (SUVmax 9.8). No contralateral nodal disease, no mediastinal or hilar nodes, no distant metastatic lesions. Lungs clear.

- Next-generation sequencing (523-gene panel): PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation (AF 18%); TERT promoter C228T mutation (AF 22%); FAT1 p.Q2401* nonsense mutation (AF 15%); EP300 p.R1627Q missense (AF 11%, VUS); NOTCH1 p.A465T missense (AF 9%, VUS). CCND1 amplification (6.4-fold); CDKN2A homozygous deletion; EGFR low-level copy number gain. No TP53, KRAS, NRAS, or BRAF mutations. No actionable fusions. MSS. TMB 6.2 mut/Mb (low). PD-L1 CPS 12 (SP142). HPV integration detected. A BRCA2 variant of uncertain significance (p.D2723H, AF ~48%) was identified and appears likely germline; germline counseling has been recommended.

Final clinical stage: T2N1M0 — by AJCC 8th edition conventional criteria this is stage III; per the AJCC 8th edition HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging (p16+ pathway), this represents pathologic/clinical stage I, carrying a substantially more favorable prognosis.

The patient now presents today for formal multidisciplinary evaluation and discussion of treatment options. She reports that the odynophagia has worsened somewhat since initial presentation; pain with swallowing is now 5/10 at rest and 7/10 with eating. She is currently managing a soft diet without difficulty maintaining her weight. She has not experienced any aspiration, frank dysphagia to liquids, respiratory symptoms, otalgia, or neurologic symptoms. She reports no new lymphadenopathy elsewhere.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Right-sided odynophagia (worsening over 3 months), right neck mass, mild fatigue since diagnosis (anxiety-related), decreased oral intake due to pain.
Negative: No fever, chills, night sweats. No unexplained weight loss (weight stable). No hoarseness or voice change. No aspiration, choking, or frank liquid dysphagia. No nasal obstruction, epistaxis, otalgia, or otitis media. No diplopia, facial numbness, or cranial nerve symptoms. No trismus. No cough or hemoptysis. No chest pain, shortness of breath. No bone pain. No skin lesions. No symptoms of hypothyroidism exacerbation.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Hypothyroidism, well-controlled — on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (TSH within normal range at most recent check)
2. No prior malignancies
3. No prior head and neck surgery or radiation
4. Remote HPV exposure (history of HPV on gynecologic screening, subtype not documented; likely related to oropharyngeal oncogenesis in this case)
5. No history of immunosuppression

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY

- Direct laryngoscopy with biopsy of right base of tongue (for above diagnosis) — uncomplicated
- No other prior surgeries

FAMILY HISTORY

- No known family history of head and neck cancers, oropharyngeal cancer, or HPV-associated malignancies.
- No known family history of breast, ovarian, or BRCA-related cancers (relevant given germline BRCA2 VUS on NGS; patient to be referred to genetics for counseling).
- Father: deceased, cardiovascular disease. Mother: alive, type 2 diabetes.
- No family history of thyroid cancer.

SOCIAL HISTORY

- Never smoker (0 pack-years); no tobacco use of any kind.
- No alcohol use.
- Works as a high school music teacher; employed full-time.
- In a stable, monogamous long-term partnership.
- West African descent; born in Ghana, immigrated to the United States as a young adult.
- Lives with partner; strong social support system.
- No illicit drug use.
- Denies recreational cannabis use.
- Functional and active baseline; ECOG performance status 0 at diagnosis.

ALLERGIES

- No known drug allergies.
- No known contrast allergies (IV contrast used for MRI/PET without event).

MEDICATIONS

1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (for hypothyroidism, well-controlled)
2. Multivitamin daily
3. No other prescription medications
4. No anticoagulants, NSAIDs, or herbals of note

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished 52-year-old woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears stated age. Mood appropriate; mild anxiety acknowledged regarding diagnosis.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, temperature 36.8°C, weight 76 kg, height 163 cm, BMI 28.6 kg/m².

Head and Neck:
- Scalp: No scalp lesions or lymphadenopathy.
- Face: Symmetric, no facial nerve weakness, no parotid masses.
- Eyes: Extraocular movements intact; no diplopia elicited.
- Ears: Canals clear bilaterally; TMs intact, no middle ear effusion.
- Nose: Patent bilaterally, no epistaxis, no obstruction.
- Oral cavity: Adequate mouth opening (approximately 4.5 cm, three fingerbreadths). Dentition present and in good repair; no dental caries requiring extraction. Tongue: midline, full mobility. Oral mucosa pink and moist, no lesions in the anterior oral cavity. Hard/soft palate: intact, no visible lesion.
- Oropharynx: A firm, exophytic lesion is palpable and partially visible at the right base of tongue, approximately 2-3 cm estimated clinically, with adjacent right tonsillar fossa without gross involvement. Uvula midline. Soft palate elevates symmetrically.
- Neck: Right level IIA — a firm, discrete, approximately 2 cm lymph node palpable in the right submandibular/jugulodigastric region. Non-tender, non-mobile (mildly fixed), no overlying skin changes. No contralateral cervical adenopathy. No bilateral posterior triangle nodes. No supraclavicular adenopathy. No midline neck mass. Thyroid: no palpable nodules, normal size.
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy (performed in office): nasopharynx clear, no nasopharyngeal mass. Oropharynx: exophytic, friable mass at the right base of tongue, extending toward the right vallecula border but without definitive epiglottic involvement on scope. Right tonsillar fossa appears involved at the inferior pole. Hypopharynx: piriform sinuses patent bilaterally, no lesion. Larynx: true vocal cords mobile bilaterally, symmetric abduction, no glottic or supraglottic lesions. Subglottis normal.

Chest: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness, no crackles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly.
Extremities: No peripheral edema, no lymphedema of upper extremities.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No facial droop. Tongue midline. No peripheral neuropathy on gross sensory exam.
Skin: No skin lesions. No signs of radiation changes (no prior treatment).

---

LAB RESULTS (recent, reviewed today)

- CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 13.4 g/dL, Plt 224 x10^9/L — within normal limits.
- BMP: Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 3.9 mEq/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL (eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m²), BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 88 mg/dL — normal renal function, no electrolyte abnormalities.
- LFTs: Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 68 U/L — normal.
- Magnesium: 2.0 mg/dL (normal).
- TSH: 2.3 mIU/L (normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg; no dose change currently needed).
- Albumin: 4.0 g/dL.
- HbA1c: Not performed (no diabetes history).
- LDH: Normal.
- HIV: Negative (tested as part of staging workup for HPV-associated SCC in younger patient).

Audiogram (baseline, obtained prior to counseling regarding cisplatin): Normal bilateral hearing thresholds across speech frequencies (250-4000 Hz). No sensorineural hearing loss at baseline.

BASELINE FEES (Functional Endoscopic Evaluation of Swallowing, performed by speech-language pathology): Mild pharyngeal phase delay noted. No aspiration on thin liquids. No penetration. Able to manage regular diet with mild mechanical modification for comfort given odynophagia. No aspiration risk on modified barium swallow with current diet.

Dental Evaluation: Completed by oral/dental oncology. No grossly decayed or compromised teeth. No extractions required prior to radiation. Dental clearance obtained. Patient counseled on fluoride trays and dental hygiene during and after radiation.

---

IMAGING (reviewed in detail)

MRI neck (staging): 3.2 x 2.8 cm enhancing right base of tongue mass; single right level IIA node 2.1 x 1.8 cm with central necrosis. No contralateral disease, no distant spread, no mandibular involvement.

PET-CT (staging): FDG-avid base of tongue primary (SUVmax 14.2); single FDG-avid right level IIA node (SUVmax 9.8). Clean elsewhere: no mediastinal, hilar, distant nodes, no liver or lung metastases.

Imaging reviewed and discussed in detail at today's tumor board.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old never-smoking woman with newly diagnosed T2N1M0, p16-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, clinical stage I by AJCC 8th edition HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging (conventional stage III). ECOG PS 0. Excellent renal function. Baseline audiogram normal. Complete dental clearance. This case was reviewed at the multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board today with representatives from medical oncology, radiation oncology, head and neck surgery, speech-language pathology, dietetics, and social work.

1. TREATMENT RECOMMENDATION — DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION (CRT)

The tumor board's unanimous recommendation is definitive concurrent chemoradiation with cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT).

Rationale:
- T2N1M0 HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC in a never-smoker is highly amenable to definitive CRT, which is the standard of care in this setting and achieves locoregional control rates of 85-90% at 5 years.
- Surgery (transoral robotic surgery, TORS) was discussed in detail. TORS followed by adjuvant radiation with or without chemotherapy could be considered, but the presence of a single positive lymph node with central necrosis (high likelihood of requiring adjuvant CRT post-operatively) makes the CRT-first approach preferable to avoid trimodality treatment and its associated cumulative toxicities. The tumor board voted against upfront surgery.
- Induction chemotherapy followed by concurrent CRT was also discussed and not recommended given the stage I HPV-positive classification and the absence of high-risk features (e.g., bilateral nodes, multiple nodes, or T4 disease) that might prompt an induction approach.
- Immunotherapy-based approaches (e.g., addition of pembrolizumab to CRT) were noted as investigational in the definitive setting; the patient's PD-L1 CPS of 12 is above the threshold of interest but there is no established role for adding checkpoint inhibitors to definitive CRT outside of a clinical trial.

Radiation Plan (per radiation oncology):
- Modality: IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique, VMAT, with daily CBCT image guidance.
- High-risk CTV (primary tumor + right level IIA node): 70 Gy in 35 fractions (2.0 Gy/fraction).
- Intermediate-risk CTV (right neck levels II-IV, bilateral retropharyngeal nodes): 63 Gy in 35 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction).
- Low-risk elective CTV (bilateral levels II-IV): 56 Gy in 35 fractions (1.6 Gy/fraction).
- Sparing goals: contralateral parotid mean dose <26 Gy, spinal cord max <45 Gy, mandible max <70 Gy.
- Treatment: 5 days per week, 7 weeks total.
- Immobilization: Thermoplastic mask with dental bite block as appropriate.

Chemotherapy Plan (per medical oncology):
- Weekly cisplatin at 40 mg/m² IV (based on BSA 1.72 m², planned dose ~69 mg IV weekly) x 7 cycles concurrent with IMRT.
- Premedication: Ondansetron 24 mg IV, dexamethasone 12 mg IV, IV magnesium sulfate 2 g, standard pre- and post-hydration with IV normal saline.
- Rationale for weekly over high-dose cisplatin (100 mg/m² q3wk): equivalent efficacy data in HPV-positive OPSCC with potentially better tolerability in this setting; institutional preference.
- Amifostine: Not planned (evidence insufficient to routinely recommend; may attenuate xerostomia but not consistently in IMRT era).

2. DE-ESCALATION TRIALS

De-escalation strategies for HPV-positive OPSCC (e.g., ECOG 3311, NRG HN002, OPTIMA) were reviewed and discussed with the patient in detail. The tumor board considered whether Ms. Osei-Bonsu would be eligible for or benefit from a de-escalation trial. Given the T2N1 staging, never-smoking status, and strongly p16-positive biology, she was felt to be a reasonable candidate for some de-escalation protocols; however, no active open trial was available at this institution for her exact disease profile at this time. The patient was counseled but will not be enrolled in a de-escalation protocol. Standard-of-care definitive CRT as described above will proceed.

3. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT — PEG TUBE

Given the expected acute toxicities of CRT (grade 3 mucositis, dysphagia, odynophagia) and the patient's baseline swallowing physiology (mild pharyngeal delay on FEES, currently symptomatic odynophagia), the multidisciplinary team discussed prophylactic versus reactive PEG tube placement. Dietetics estimates caloric requirements during treatment at approximately 2200 kcal/day. There is a significant risk of nutritional compromise necessitating enteral support during treatment. After extensive discussion, the team recommends proceeding with prophylactic PEG placement prior to initiation of CRT. The preference will be for the patient to continue oral intake as long as tolerated, with PEG use reserved for supplemental or complete nutritional support as needed during treatment. Interventional gastroenterology has been contacted for pre-treatment PEG placement scheduling.

4. SWALLOWING REHABILITATION

Speech-language pathology will follow the patient intensively before, during, and after treatment. The patient has been given pre-treatment swallowing exercises (Mendelsohn maneuver, effortful swallow, Shaker exercises) to maintain swallowing muscle mass and function during treatment. Goal is to minimize post-CRT dysphagia and duration of PEG dependency.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL / SOCIAL WORK

The patient expressed anxiety and distress about the diagnosis and its impact on her professional role as a music teacher (voice-dependent occupation). She was connected with the head and neck oncology social worker for psychological support, cancer-related distress screening, and community resource navigation. Referral to the head and neck survivorship program was made for long-term planning.

6. MOLECULAR / GENOMIC FINDINGS

NGS results were reviewed. PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation is present and noted as potentially actionable; however, there are no approved PIK3CA-targeted agents for head and neck SCC in the definitive setting, and this finding will be kept in mind for potential future use if needed. CCND1 amplification and CDKN2A deletion are noted as consistent with the biology of HPV-positive OPSCC and do not alter current treatment planning. TMB of 6.2 mut/Mb is low; MSS status confirmed. PD-L1 CPS 12 — not currently impacting definitive treatment selection (no role for PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in definitive CRT at present per standard guidelines). The BRCA2 p.D2723H variant (allele frequency ~48%, likely germline) has been flagged; germline genetic counseling referral has been placed. This is unlikely to affect treatment of the current SCC but may have implications for breast/ovarian cancer risk counseling.

7. ADDITIONAL PRE-TREATMENT WORKUP

- Audiogram: Completed (normal; baseline established prior to cisplatin).
- Creatinine / renal function: Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, eGFR >90 — adequate for weekly cisplatin dosing.
- Dental clearance: Obtained.
- FEES: Completed — no aspiration, mild pharyngeal delay.
- HIV: Negative.
- Thyroid function: TSH 2.3 mIU/L, euthyroid on levothyroxine. Thyroid to be closely monitored during and after neck radiation (radiation-induced hypothyroidism is expected and anticipated over months to years post-treatment).

8. PATIENT COUNSELING AND CONSENT

Extensive counseling was provided regarding:
- Acute toxicities: grade 2-3 mucositis (near-universal), severe odynophagia and dysphagia, skin reaction (radiation dermatitis), xerostomia, dysgeusia, fatigue, weight loss, alopecia (limited to radiation field), nausea, and the likely need for PEG tube feeds during treatment.
- Cisplatin-specific toxicities: nephrotoxicity (close creatinine monitoring), ototoxicity (audiogram monitoring), nausea/vomiting, myelosuppression, peripheral neuropathy (less common at weekly dosing).
- Late effects: chronic xerostomia, dysphagia, trismus (rare but possible), hypothyroidism (expected), potential for carotid stenosis (long-term), potential for osteoradionecrosis of mandible (low risk given sparing), risk of secondary malignancies (low but real).
- Expected outcomes: Excellent prognosis given HPV-positive histology, never-smoking status, and T2N1 disease. Estimated 5-year overall survival ~85-90% in this group with complete metabolic response.

The patient verbalized understanding of the treatment plan, risks, benefits, and alternatives. She provided written informed consent for concurrent cisplatin-IMRT and agreed to PEG tube placement. She also consented to genetic counseling referral.

ECOG PS: 0 at the time of treatment planning.

CANCER PRESENT. Treatment initiation planned within the next 2 weeks pending PEG placement and final radiation treatment planning simulation.

---

PLAN SUMMARY:
- Proceed with prophylactic PEG tube placement (interventional GI referral placed)
- Initiate IMRT simulation / treatment planning (radiation oncology)
- Initiate concurrent weekly cisplatin (medical oncology)
- Speech-language pathology follow throughout treatment
- Dietetics follow throughout treatment
- Social work engaged
- Genetics referral placed (BRCA2 VUS)
- Return to clinic: 1 week after treatment initiation for toxicity assessment; then weekly during CRT

Documented by: Medical Oncology / Head and Neck Oncology, co-signed by Radiation Oncology
Multidisciplinary participants: Medical Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Head and Neck Surgery, Speech-Language Pathology, Dietetics, Social Work
